poster_title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Obscures Hallucination Cues in Large Language Models: An Empirical Evaluation
authors: Jiahao Cheng, Tiancheng Su, Jia Yuan, Guoxiu He, Jiawei Liu, Xinqi Tao, Jingwen Xie, Huaxia Li
paper_info: East China Normal University, Wuhan University, Xiaohongshu Inc., 2025

content: LLMs excel in NLP tasks but suffer from hallucinations.
Hallucination detection methods rely on output consistency, internal states, and self-evaluation.
Impact of CoT prompting on hallucination detection is under-explored.
CoT prompting may obscure critical signals for hallucination detection.
This can impair the effectiveness of existing detection methods.
Need to evaluate the robustness of detection methods under CoT prompting.
Pilot experiments on MCQA tasks: CommonSenseQA, ARC-Challenge, MMLU.
Comprehensive evaluations on complex QA and summarization datasets.
Evaluated three CoT prompting strategies and four hallucination detection methods.
Used three MCQA datasets: CommonSenseQA, ARC-Challenge, MMLU.
Evaluated three LLMs: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-v0.3-Instruct, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B.
Assessed changes in hallucination score distributions, detection accuracy, and confidence.
CoT prompting alters the probability distribution of final answer tokens.
Consistency-based methods show greater robustness; internal-state and self-evaluation methods are more susceptible.
Advanced detection methods lose their advantage over perplexity-based baselines under CoT prompting.
CoT prompting enhances LLM performance but disrupts hallucination detection mechanisms.
Reasoning-enhanced inference makes hallucination signals more subtle and harder to capture.
Future work should explore generalizability across tasks and domains.
